Disciplining God: The Meaning of Sovereignty

Maahi Gupta · NYU Center for Global Affairs

GitHub Portfolio
About
Maahi Gupta is a graduate student at NYU's Center for Global Affairs, working across global risk, international security, and political theory. At some point in the last six months, sovereignty took over. Winston is what happened when she decided to make it shareable.
I have been working on this thesis for roughly six months. At some point it stopped being a project and started being the way I saw everything. Sovereignty was in every conversation, every conflict, every headline. To avoid speaking an alien language to everyone I love, I decided to find a way to make it heard.

Winston is for everyone who cared enough to see this through with me. You can finally see what I have been up to. And in some hopeful part of me, Winston is also for whoever finds this while working in the same territory. I hope it is useful. I hope it finds the right person.
The thesis
The contemporary definition of sovereignty, grounded in the Westphalian model and institutionalized through the United Nations, treats political authority as an intrinsic and territorially bounded property of the state.
This thesis argues that this conception is fundamentally misaligned with how sovereignty actually operates. In its place, it proposes the following definition: sovereignty is a continuous system in which an organizing body claims, in relation to another, the dynamic capacity to discipline and control the material and symbolic conditions necessary for its own reproduction over time.

This reconceptualization reframes sovereignty as a relational, reproductive process rather than a static condition, with significant implications for how international institutions understand and respond to political conflict.
Thesis advisor
Thesis supervised by Dr. Christopher Ankersen, Clinical Professor at NYU's Center for Global Affairs, whose background spans the UN, the Canadian Forces, and the London School of Economics.

I'm Winston, an AI built to think about sovereignty.
What it actually is. How it actually works.
Why the framework we've been using to understand it is broken.

Ask me anything.

Why did Maahi choose this topic?
The Diagnostic Framework

Zero-Sum vs Cooperative Sovereignty

This framework maps any conflict by asking one question: are the conditions of reproduction structurally shared or structurally incompatible? The answer determines whether institutional intervention can work, or whether it is being directed at a contest that cannot be resolved, only managed.

Zero-Sum
Palestine / Israel
Reproduction of one identity requires the erasure of another. Structurally irresolvable.
Cooperative
Smallpox Eradication
US and USSR supplied 1.4bn doses jointly. No state could act alone. Eradicated 1979.
Zero-Sum
Ukraine / Russia
Symbolic destruction as sovereign strategy. Heritage sites, archives, memory as targets.
Cooperative
Climate Governance
No state can reproduce survival conditions alone. Shared material conditions drive cooperation.
How the Framework Works

The diagnostic tool in three steps

Step one: identify the organizing body and its conditions of reproduction. What material conditions does it require to persist over time? What symbolic conditions, the myths, narratives, languages, and memory structures, does it require to recognize itself as continuous?

Step two: identify what is being contested. Are the material conditions under threat? Are the symbolic conditions under systematic attack? Is the challenge zero-sum, where one body's reproduction requires another's erasure, or is it cooperative, where conditions are genuinely shared?

Step three: direct resources accordingly. Zero-sum contests cannot be resolved through institutional means. They can only be managed. Cooperative domains can be addressed. The diagnostic tool tells you which domain you are in before you spend the money.

This is not a prediction engine. It is a resource allocation tool. The goal is alignment between the nature of the problem and the design of the response. (Gupta, "Disciplining God," 2026)
Sovereignty is a continuous system in which an organizing body claims, in relation to another, the dynamic capacity to discipline and control the material and symbolic conditions necessary for its own reproduction over time. Discipline is the ongoing governance of meaning, behavior, and narrative. Control is the actual possession and management of resources. Continuous is load-bearing: a sovereignty that pauses is already weakening.
Select a case study
What makes a domain cooperative?

When sovereignty is not zero-sum

The Westphalian model treats all sovereignty as competitive. This framework distinguishes. When the conditions of reproduction are genuinely shared, meaning no organizing body can secure them independently, cooperation is not idealism. It is the only mechanism through which any party can reproduce the conditions of its own existence.

The diagnostic question is structural: can one organizing body secure this condition without the other? If no, the domain is cooperative. If yes, it is contested. The answer determines whether institutional resources should go toward coordination or management.

Smallpox Eradication (1967–1979)

The clearest case in modern history

By 1979, smallpox had been completely eradicated through a WHO-led campaign in which the United States and Soviet Union cooperated directly, jointly supplying over 1.4 billion vaccine doses at the height of Cold War hostility. The Westphalian model cannot explain this. If sovereignty is territorially competitive, why would adversaries cooperate at this scale?

The answer is structural. No state could secure its population from a transnational pathogen independently. The conditions of biological reproduction were genuinely shared. Cooperation was not idealism. It was the only mechanism through which each state could reproduce the conditions of its own existence. (Bristol, CSIS, 2023; UN International Health Regulations)

1.4BVaccine doses (US + USSR joint)
1979Year of eradication
0States that could do it alone
UNDP in China — Norm Diffusion as Symbolic Sovereignty (Lu, 2019)

How the UN reproduces its own symbolic conditions inside member states

Xiaoyu Lu's political ethnography of the UNDP China office (Oxford DPhil, 2019) documents how international norms travel not through formal treaties but through storytelling in liminal institutional spaces. UNDP staff translated global human rights frameworks into local "cases" and "experiences" to make them legible to Chinese government partners. The norms were neither purely global nor purely local. They were entangled.

This is directly relevant to this thesis. The UNDP's authority in China was not derived from the Westphalian model. It was derived from its capacity to discipline the symbolic conditions through which Chinese partners understood what counted as legitimate development. UNDP was exercising a form of sovereignty without territory: it was controlling the narrative conditions through which international norms became locally actionable.

Lu shows that norm diffusion fails when organizations treat it as top-down transmission of formal texts. It succeeds when actors translate norms into stories, cases, and experiences that resonate with the recipient's existing symbolic toolkit. This is Swidler's cultural toolkit operating at institutional scale. (Lu, "Neither Local nor Global," Oxford, 2019)

Implication for UN allocation: resources directed at formal treaty compliance go largely unread. Resources directed at local storytelling, case documentation, and narrative translation produce durable norm adoption. The symbolic conditions of institutional legitimacy must be actively reproduced, not assumed.
Climate Governance — Shared Material Conditions

Why climate is structurally cooperative

No state can reproduce the material conditions of its own survival independently from a changing climate. The atmospheric commons is the clearest case of genuinely shared conditions of reproduction in the contemporary international system. This is why climate governance has produced sustained institutional cooperation despite geopolitical antagonism: the alternative is not competition, it is collective failure.

The Paris Agreement is not idealism. It is what cooperation looks like when conditions of reproduction are structurally shared. The challenge is that individual states still attempt to defect from cooperative obligations when short-term material interests diverge. The framework predicts this: shared conditions do not eliminate contested claims over the terms of cooperation. They only change the domain from irresolvable to addressable.

The Blackrock Problem

Why standard sovereign risk analysis is a lagging indicator

Standard sovereign risk models price political risk using Westphalian indicators: credit ratings, regime stability, territorial control, formal legal frameworks. These are lagging indicators. They measure the formal surface of sovereignty, not the underlying capacity for reproduction. A state can hold all Westphalian markers and still be losing sovereignty rapidly, because its symbolic conditions are eroding faster than any credit agency can detect.

Sri Lanka's 2022 default is the empirical case. Standard models rated it investment grade as late as 2019. The symbolic indicators were legible from 2019: institutional delegitimization, political identity fracture under Rajapaksa governance, collapse of public trust in state reproduction capacity. Three years of legible warning went unread because the analytical framework had no category for it.

Sri Lanka 2022 — The Gap

What symbolic indicators showed vs what credit ratings showed

$51BExternal debt at default
$2.9BIMF emergency bailout
2019When symbolic signals broke
Westphalian signal (credit rating) Symbolic sovereignty signal
The gap between 2019 and 2022 is not theoretical. It is 2–3 years of investment exposure at investment-grade pricing that the symbolic indicators would have flagged as structurally fragile. (IMF Article IV Consultations 2019–2022)

The intellectual development behind Winston

Three projects, one argument

Winston is the third in a sequence of research projects, each of which approaches the same underlying question from a different angle: how do people reproduce their identities over time, under what conditions does that reproduction become contested, and what happens when the institutions designed to support it fail to see what they are actually dealing with?

The first project was a medical anthropology study of doctors during the Covid pandemic. The second was an ethnographic study of a Zumba studio in Delhi. The third is this thesis. None of them were planned as a sequence. But looking back, each one was already asking the same question in a different register.

2023
medical anthropology

"Death in Covid: An Intersection between the Biomedical, Social and Political"

Shiv Nadar University OUR. Supervised by Dr. Ravi Nandan Singh. Fieldwork at university health centres and hospitals across Delhi NCR and Mumbai during and after the COVID-19 pandemic.

The paper examined how medical professionals developed a "gaze" toward the pandemic through successive waves. Key finding: the clinical gaze, like the Westphalian gaze, is a lagging indicator. Doctors did not see the pandemic as a pandemic until the second wave. The markers they used to understand it, death tolls, wave patterns, variant classifications, were material and formal. The symbolic conditions of patient experience, gendered professional responsibility, the deification and skepticism of doctors, the residue of pandemic protocols in hospital architecture, were what the paper tracked instead.

The connection to the sovereignty thesis: Foucault's governmentality appears in both papers. The clinical gaze and the Westphalian gaze operate by the same logic. Both see formal surfaces and miss the underlying conditions. Medical pluralism during the pandemic, the simultaneous authority claims of WHO, ICMR, and Patanjali Ayurveda, is a direct case of multiple organizing bodies contesting the right to define what is real. (Gupta, "Death in Covid," Shiv Nadar University, 2023)

Foucault · Canguilhem · Atkinson · Naraindas · de Beauvoir · Butler
2024
sociology / ethnography

"Intersections of Dance and Exercise in Zumba"

Shiv Nadar University BA Research thesis. Supervised by Dr. Tuhina Ganguly. Three weeks of participant observation at Rhythm Studio, North-West Delhi. Evening classes, 12–15 participants per session.

The thesis asked why people leave their homes and move their bodies to music. What it found was the constitution of the self through practice: Foucault's technologies of the self operating inside a Zumba class. The Bollywoodization of Zumba, replacing Latin beats with the Hanuman Chalisa, Punjabi wedding songs, and Bollywood item numbers, showed how a global form absorbs local symbolic conditions. The studio became a liminal space where women could transgress social roles, wear fluorescent pink, smoke cigarettes afterward, and call it all an "addiction" that made them feel free.

The connection to the sovereignty thesis: Mauss' bodily techniques are socially produced, exactly as symbolic conditions of reproduction are socially produced. Bourdieu's habitus appears in both. The studio is a sovereignty argument at microscale: identity reproduction requires a continuous practice, a space, a set of symbolic resources. When those resources change, as they did when Zumba absorbed Bollywood, the identity reproduction changes with them. Bollywoodization is not merely cultural borrowing. It is the disciplining of symbolic conditions across a global form. (Gupta, "Intersections of Dance and Exercise in Zumba," Shiv Nadar University, 2024)

Mauss · Bourdieu · Foucault · Chakravorty · Wacquant · Ben Anderson
2026
MA thesis · NYU Center for Global Affairs

"Disciplining God: The Meaning of Sovereignty"

Supervised by Dr. Christopher Ankersen. The thesis that became Winston.

The thesis began with Palestine. That question, whose claim to land counts and why, collapsed the Westphalian model almost immediately. What replaced it was a definition built from the ground up: sovereignty is a continuous system in which an organizing body claims, in relation to another, the dynamic capacity to discipline and control the material and symbolic conditions necessary for its own reproduction over time.

Looking back, this definition was already implicit in the Zumba thesis. The studio reproduced an identity. The music disciplined the symbolic conditions. The habitus structured who could move freely and who could not. And the Covid paper had already found that institutional frameworks, clinical or sovereign, miss the conditions that actually drive fragility, reproduction, and collapse.

God is in the title because God is what organizes a people when material conditions fail them. And when God dies, as Nietzsche said, what dies is the entire symbolic infrastructure through which a people organized its understanding of what is real, what is just, and who belongs. That is sovereignty. And it was always there, in the studio in Delhi, and in the hospitals of Mumbai.

Walker · Cox · Foucault · Bourdieu · Latour · Arendt · Levi-Strauss · Mitzen · Swidler · Krasner · Kallman · Nietzsche
This is still a developing project.

Questions about any of these projects, or about how they connect, belong in a conversation with Maahi directly.

maahigupta@gmail.com
UN RESOURCE ALLOCATION · SYSTEM OVERVIEW · 2023
Where institutional spending
meets the sovereignty framework

The UN spent $68.5 billion in 2023. The largest shares went to SDG 16 (Peace, Justice: $12.1B), SDG 2 (Zero Hunger: $11.8B), and SDG 3 (Health: $9.1B). The lowest allocations went to environmental sustainability goals. This distribution reflects the Westphalian model on which the UN was built: it can see material crises, respond to food insecurity, fund peacekeeping, address disease. It cannot see the symbolic conditions that produce those crises.

Humanitarian + Peace (material crisis response) Development + Global agenda (symbolic + structural)
SDG Spending vs Symbolic Conditions

What the SDG categories cannot see

The SDG framework tracks 17 goals and 169 targets. SDG 16 is "Peace, Justice, and Strong Institutions." In 2023, it received $12.1 billion, of which 60% went to UN-DPO peacekeeping operations. The remainder went to political missions and UNDP governance support. Almost none of it went to what this thesis identifies as the actual drivers of sovereign fragility: the symbolic conditions of institutional legitimacy, cultural coherence, narrative infrastructure, and political identity reproduction.

The misalignment is structural. The SDG framework was built on the Westphalian model. It can count peacekeeping troops. It cannot count the degree to which a state's governing myth retains its hold on the population it claims to represent.

Data gap notice: this analysis uses 2023 UN CEB financial reporting. Project-level SDG coding, entity-level outcome indicators, and OCHA Financial Tracking Service crisis-level data would allow Winston to make these claims with greater empirical precision. (UN CEB Financial Statistics 2023; Gupta, "Disciplining God," 2026)

Top donor governments, 2026 reporting

Funding tracked through the OCHA Financial Tracking Service. The same donors who fund material crisis response rarely fund the symbolic conditions, cultural infrastructure, narrative legitimacy, identity reproduction, that would prevent the crisis from recurring.

UN Resource Allocation — Misalignment Diagnostic

Where material spending failed to address symbolic causes

Select a conflict below to see where resources were directed, where the symbolic conditions were left unfunded, and what the misalignment cost. Counterfactual redirect estimates are framework-derived, not claims of audited UN savings. Where Winston does not have enough credible data, he will say so.

Peacekeeping ROI — What Did the Money Buy?

The diagnostic question peacekeeping cannot ask itself

UN peacekeeping spent approximately $5–6 billion per year throughout the 2010s and 2020s. By the framework in this thesis, the critical question is not whether peacekeeping achieved stability, it rarely does that, but whether the money was directed at the conditions that were actually producing the conflict. In every case below, the answer is the same: peacekeeping addressed material security conditions while the symbolic conditions producing the conflict went institutionally unaddressed.

Cross-Mission Comparison

The chart below compares annual costs against mission duration for the four major peacekeeping missions. None achieved the resolution of the underlying conflict. All addressed material security conditions. None were designed to address symbolic sovereignty conditions.

Annual cost (USD bn, approx) Years active
Sources: UN DPO Approved Budgets (published annually at peacekeeping.un.org); SIPRI Multilateral Peace Operations Database; UN Secretary-General Mission Reports. Note: figures are approximate and reflect publicly available budget data. Project-level outcome data would strengthen this analysis further.
Entity Deep Dives — Reading the Institutional Gaze
thesis genealogy

How the thesis became itself

This is not a separate argument. It is the intellectual trail: Palestine as the initial political wound, Westphalia as the inherited grammar, Arendt as the bridge to authority, God as the hardest symbolic case, and the UN allocation tool as the practical consequence. Click each node to watch the argument mutate.

clickable mindmap
Disciplining God
Sovereignty as the continuous reproduction of material and symbolic conditions over time.
origin
The question that started it
what changed
what remained
advisor interventions

How Dr. Ankersen’s comments changed the thesis

The comments are not separate from the argument. They show the moment the thesis stopped being a dense conceptual essay and became a usable diagnostic instrument. Dr. Ankersen kept asking the questions that make the framework work: what is the object, where does this concept belong, what is the transition, what are the examples, and what does this let institutions do differently?

clarify the object
From interesting theory to precise target
Early comments pressed the opening to say whether it was about a pre-modern inheritance, a contemporary model, or a broader grammar of authority. This forced the project to name Westphalia as an inherited institutional lens, not just a historical reference.
tighten the mechanism
From concept pile to causal chain
The marginal questions around habitus, myths, symbolic authority, and "what does this consist of?" pushed the thesis to explain how symbolic conditions actually reproduce political identity over time.
force the implication
From critique to allocation tool
The comments asking for examples, shared sovereignty, and how institutions should use the framework became the practical end of Winston: mapping material and symbolic conditions so resources are not misallocated.
1. pressure
What is this really about?
→
2. rebuild
Authority became the mechanism.
→
3. test
Cases forced the definition to work.
→
4. use
UN allocation became the institutional payoff.
source fragments, optional view

The thesis journey stays clean by default. Open these only if you want to see the raw notebook pages and marked-up drafts behind the mindmap. They are evidence of process, not formal citations.

Show Maahi’s notebook fragments
These pages trace the informal thinking: recognition, myths, God, sovereignty as continuous reproduction. Some handwriting is partial, so Winston treats them as fragments rather than authoritative transcript.
Notebook origin. Early questions about recognition and the visibility of sovereign claims.
Notebook God section. God begins to function as symbolic infrastructure, not theology.
Show Dr. Ankersen’s marked-up interventions
These comments are the conceptual scaffolding behind the final version. They press the introduction, the God argument, the myth/habitus section, the implications section, and the institutional use-case.
Opening pressure. Comments ask what the modern understanding is, what Gross actually shows, and why natural law has to be introduced clearly.
Framework pressure. The margin asks for the analytical framework and says the argument must be made more precise, not merely asserted.
Myth and reality. The comment to insert myths here helped make symbolic reproduction a central mechanism rather than a side theme.
Implications. Notes ask for examples and warn against overloaded discussion, which sharpened the move from theory to institutional use.
Shared sovereignty. Questions like "what does this mean?" and "why shared?" pushed the UN allocation logic toward domains where conditions of survival are genuinely shared.
Operational method. The margin says this needs its own heading, which became the interdisciplinary mapping framework behind Winston.
about this project

Winston is a thesis made conversational.

This is a developing project. Winston represents Maahi Gupta's MA thesis at NYU's Center for Global Affairs. He can reason about sovereignty, analyze cases, and map UN resource allocation through the lens of this framework. He cannot do everything yet, and he will tell you when he cannot.

Reach the author directly: maahigupta@gmail.com
✦
the author

Maahi Gupta

MA student at NYU's Center for Global Affairs, working across global risk, international security, and political theory. This thesis began with a question about Palestine and Israel: whose claim to land counts, and why? That question collapsed the Westphalian model almost immediately. So she rebuilt it.

Winston is what happened when she decided to make the argument shareable. It is also, in the words of the thesis itself: her act of sovereignty.

✦
thesis advisor

Dr. Christopher Ankersen

Clinical Professor at NYU's Center for Global Affairs, where he leads the Global Risk Specialization. MSc and PhD from the London School of Economics. Worked at the United Nations from 2005 to 2017. Twelve years as an officer in the Canadian Forces.

His challenges and interventions throughout the drafting process were instrumental in sharpening the thesis's conceptual architecture. Every weekly meeting ended with more books to read.

✦
what Winston can do

Capabilities

  • Explain and defend the thesis definition of sovereignty
  • Apply the material and symbolic conditions framework to any case
  • Analyze UN resource allocation patterns against the framework
  • Map zero-sum versus cooperative sovereignty domains
  • Perform sovereign risk analysis in the Blackrock framing
  • Engage theorists: Walker, Cox, Foucault, Arendt, Bourdieu, Latour, Levi-Strauss, Mitzen, Swidler, Krasner, Kallman
  • Read symbolic conditions the way an anthropologist would
  • Cite only verified sources; refuse to speculate when data is missing
✦
what Winston cannot do yet

Limitations

  • Access real-time news or events after his knowledge cutoff
  • Perform project-level UN expenditure analysis without project data
  • Generate procurement or audit-level financial analysis
  • Produce country-specific implementation dashboards without country datasets
  • Make empirical claims without credible sources to ground them
  • Replace a conversation with Maahi Gupta herself
This is a developing project. Winston will grow as the underlying data does.
how to use Winston
diagnose a case
"Map Pakistan's material and symbolic conditions of reproduction."
"Apply the framework to Yemen. Is it zero-sum or cooperative?"
"What does this definition reveal about Myanmar that the Westphalian model cannot?"
analyze UN spending

"Compare WFP and UNDP spending against the sovereignty framework."
investment and risk
"How would Blackrock use this framework to price Sri Lanka's sovereign risk in 2019?"
"What symbolic indicators would have flagged Pakistan's fragility before the IMF needed to intervene?"
"Build a risk assessment for a country of my choosing."
explore the thesis
"Why does the thesis start with Palestine?"
"What is God doing in a thesis about sovereignty?"
"How does Bourdieu's habitus explain why Meta became a sovereign actor?"
data you can provide

V-Dem, Freedom House, or similar political legitimacy datasets
Project-level expenditure reports from any UN entity
Country-specific audit or evaluation reports
ethnographic sources Winston uses
Khan et al. (2020) — linguistic ethnography of Pakistan, identity fracture
Millar (2014) — ethnographic approach to peacebuilding evaluation
Watts (2001) — development ethnographies, authoritative knowledge production
Kallman (2023) — symbolic destruction in Ukraine
source discipline
Winston makes analytical claims only from credible sources: United Nations documents and datasets, Reuters, official government websites, published academic sources, and data uploaded by the author. When asked for a case study Winston does not have reliable information for, he will say so and explain what data is missing. He does not speculate dressed up as analysis.
This is still a developing project.

I encourage you to speak to me directly. Winston is a representation of an argument, not a replacement for a conversation.

maahigupta@gmail.com
These are the sources Winston is trained on. Every claim Winston makes without a citation is grounded in this bibliography. When you ask Winston to cite, it draws from here.
Primary source
Gupta, Maahi — "Disciplining God: The Meaning of Sovereignty"
Master's Thesis, NYU Center for Global Affairs, April 2026. Score: 99/100, Distinction. Winner, NYU SPS Best Thesis/Capstone Award. Supervisor: Dr. Christopher Ankersen. Winston is the AI instantiation of this thesis.
Foundational Theory
Arendt, Hannah
"What Is Authority?" Between Past and Future
Penguin Books, 2006. Authority as recognized legitimacy, neither coercion nor persuasion. The conceptual foundation for how sovereign claims become sustainable over time.
core
Bourdieu, Pierre
"Structures, Habitus, Practices." Rethinking the Subject
Routledge, 2018. Sovereignty as habitus: continuously produced, not intrinsically possessed. Explains how Meta accumulated capital sufficient to restructure its field.
core
Foucault, Michel
"Truth and Power." Power/Knowledge
Pantheon Books, 1980. Regimes of truth and governmental rationality. Sovereignty presents itself as intrinsic; Foucault reveals it as a historical artifact of disciplinary production.
core
Latour, Bruno
We Have Never Been Modern
Harvard University Press, 2012. Actor-Network Theory: sovereignty does not pre-exist its network of relations. "An actor is what is made to act by many others."
core
Walker, R. B. J.
Inside/Outside: International Relations as Political Theory
Cambridge University Press, 1993. The inside/outside distinction is a political resolution, not a description of reality. Sovereignty produces exclusion as its condition of existence.
core
Krasner, Stephen D.
Sovereignty: Organized Hypocrisy
Princeton University Press, 1999. Rules of non-intervention coexist with routine violations. Provides empirical grounding for the critique of the Westphalian model.
core
Political Theory + Philosophy
Nietzsche, Friedrich
The Gay Science
Translated by Walter Kaufmann, Vintage Books, 1974. "God is dead" — the collapse of intrinsic symbolic authority reveals that all such authority is historically produced. The thesis's title follows from this logic.
Cox, Robert W.
"Social Forces, States and World Orders"
Millennium: Journal of International Studies, vol. 10, no. 2, 1981. "Theory is always for someone and for some purpose." Problem-solving theory selected Westphalia for administrative utility, not analytical truth.
Mitzen, Jennifer
"Ontological Security in World Politics"
European Journal of International Relations, vol. 12, no. 3, 2006. States, like individuals, require a consistent narrative of selfhood across time. Sovereignty functions to secure this ontological continuity.
Flemming, Sean
Leviathan on a Leash: A Theory of State Responsibility
Princeton University Press, 2020. State responsibility and agential identity. As algorithms make state decisions, the anthropomorphized Pufendorfian state becomes untenable.
Anthropology + Culture Theory
Swidler, Ann
"Culture in Action: Symbols and Strategies"
American Sociological Review, vol. 51, no. 2, 1986. Culture as toolkit: myths and narratives are deployed strategically, not internalized as fixed values. Central to Winston's analysis of sovereign myth-making.
core
Lévi-Strauss, Claude
"The Structural Study of Myth"
The Journal of American Folklore, vol. 68, no. 270, 1955. Myths organize meaning rather than describe empirical truth. They are the symbolic infrastructure through which reality is stabilized and made collectively legible.
Khan, Imdad Ullah et al.
Linguistic ethnography of Pakistan (Ilkogretim Online, 2020)
Documents generational fracture in Pakistan's symbolic toolkit. The grandmother's designation of English as "the language of the English people" versus the granddaughter's fluency: not a generation gap but a fracture in the conditions of national identity reproduction.
empirical
Empirical Cases
Kallman, Jacob
"Russia's War on Heritage: How Attacking the Past Targets Ukraine's Future"
Transnational Security, 2023. Documents the systematic targeting of Ukrainian cultural heritage as a strategy of sovereign destruction — severing an organizing body's capacity to recognize itself as continuous.
empirical
UN Independent Fact-Finding Mission on Myanmar
Report A/HRC/39/64
UN Human Rights Council, Sept. 2018. Facebook's amplification of anti-Rohingya content played a determining role in incitement bearing hallmarks of genocidal intent. The architecture built to protect privacy is simultaneously the infrastructure through which population governance becomes technically possible.
empirical
Haugen, Frances
Senate Subcommittee Statement, 4 Oct. 2021
Facebook's internal research confirmed algorithmic amplification of harm; leadership chose not to intervene to protect revenue. The governance decision is not the harm — it is the prior choice about whose conditions of reproduction are worth protecting.
empirical
Canadian Human Rights Tribunal
Canadian Human Rights Tribunal
2019 CHRT 39 — First Nations Child and Family Services / Jordan's Principle
Framework for quantifying symbolic harm. $40,000 per child for pain and suffering from removal; separate compensation for parents and grandparents. Established that harm to cultural identity, language transmission, and intergenerational knowledge constitutes measurable legal injury. Elder and Knowledge Keeper recommendations of linguistic/cultural resources treated as legally cognizable. Used by Winston to operationalize symbolic conditions as real, measurable harm.
institutional
UN + International Institutions
United Nations
Charter of the United Nations, 26 June 1945
Article 2(1): "the Organization is based on the principle of the sovereign equality of all its Members." The structural constraint the thesis exposes.
United Nations General Assembly
Official Records of UNSCOP, 1947
Moshe Shertok's testimony frames sovereignty as material and symbolic necessity: "a matter of life and death," requiring control over "machinery of Government" and a distinct "national culture." Aligns directly with the thesis's definition.
IMF
Article IV Consultations (Sri Lanka, Pakistan, 2019–2024)
Empirical grounding for Winston's sovereign risk analysis. Sri Lanka investment-grade as late as 2019; Pakistan's recurring FX crises and EFF 2024 ($7B). The gap between Westphalian indicators and symbolic fragility.
empirical
